summary:
The Khiamian (or Khiam) culture is an Epipaleolithic to early Neolithic transitional archaeological horizon of the southern Levant, dated to approximately 10,000–9,500 BCE, positioned between the earlier [[event:4|Natufian culture]] and the [[event:1304|Pre-Pottery Neolithic A]]. It is named after the site of El Khiam in the Judean Desert near the Dead Sea, where characteristic notched arrowheads known as El-Khiam points were first identified.

The Khiamian is defined primarily by its distinctive stone tool assemblage, particularly the El-Khiam point — a small, finely worked bifacial arrowhead with a distinctive basal notch that facilitated hafting. Communities of the Khiamian period occupied the Jordan Valley and surrounding uplands, subsisting through hunting and intensive plant harvesting that was beginning the transition toward cultivation. The Khiamian represents the moment of transition between the mobile Natufian foragers and the first truly sedentary farming communities of the PPNA, bridging a critical period when permanent settlement was being established but the full Neolithic agricultural package had not yet crystallized.
